---
title: "What I Learned from Beyond p < 0.05"
format: html
---

## What I Learned from *Moving to a World Beyond “p < 0.05”* and *Abandon Statistical Significance*

After reading *Moving to a World Beyond “p < 0.05”* and *Abandon Statistical Significance*, I understood more clearly how statistical inference is different from scientific research. These two articles argue that using **p < 0.05** as the main standard for judging research is too simple and can even hurt research quality.

Right now, **p < 0.05** is often treated as strong evidence for a scientific idea. It is not only used for publication, but also used to decide whether a result is worth taking seriously. In other words, statistical significance has become a rule: a result must first pass the **p < 0.05** threshold before people pay attention to other factors. Those other factors are often treated as less important. They include prior evidence, whether the mechanism makes sense, study design, data quality, real-world costs and benefits, novelty, and other background factors that depend on the field. The authors call these **currently subordinate factors**.

One of the most important ideas I learned from these articles is that **statistical inference is not the same as scientific inference**. A p-value cannot tell us whether a hypothesis is true, whether an effect is important, or whether a result will be replicated in the future. It is only one piece of information. The authors say that researchers should not focus only on the p-value. They should also think about effect size, uncertainty, prior evidence, plausibility of mechanism, study design, data quality, and the real-world cost of making a wrong decision. This idea is meaningful to me because it moves attention away from chasing one threshold and back to the real research question.

The articles also remind us that, although a p-value is formally defined as the probability of seeing data at least as extreme as what we observed if the null hypothesis is true, many people misunderstand it. Some people think it means the probability that the null hypothesis is true, the probability that the alternative hypothesis is true, or **1 minus the probability of replication**. Also, once we use a threshold, evidence gets divided into categories in an artificial way. As Gelman and Stern pointed out, **“the difference between ‘significant’ and ‘not significant’ is not itself statistically significant.”** The authors describe this way of thinking as a kind of “statistical alchemy.” It gives people a false feeling of certainty, as if crossing a threshold means we can confidently say there is “an effect” or “no effect.”

For this reason, the authors think it is better to treat p-values as continuous numbers instead of using strict cutoffs. But they go even further. They argue that p-values usually should not be the main tool for judging evidence at all. Instead, p-values should be treated as only one part of the evidence, together with the currently subordinate factors. They also suggest that researchers should use these factors to guide data collection, statistical analysis, interpretation, writing, and other research decisions.

I think this idea is hard to achieve in real life. Maybe because of my academic background, no one told me during my undergraduate public health training that **statistical significance should not be the main standard for judging research quality**. I did not learn this idea until I entered my master’s program in biostatistics. Before that, I always felt that teachers cared a lot about p-values. For example, one of my excellent classmates was asked in a graduate school interview to explain what a p-value is. I also used to think that a result with **p < 0.05** was meaningful, while a result with **p > 0.05** was not important.

After reading these two articles, I want to change the way I think. From now on, I want to accept uncertainty more and approach research with more thoughtfulness, openness, and humility. Instead of seeing research as a simple story of either “finding an effect” or “finding no effect,” I want to see it as a learning process that is more honest about limits, variation, and uncertainty.

These articles helped me rethink the role of statistics in science. They reminded me that what really matters is not just getting a small p-value, but understanding data and the world in a fuller and more honest way.
